entry.title: E2E Use Case: Realizing a Data Product Architecture with SAP IT
entry.content:
Our mission in enterprise analytics is to empower every SAP employee to make AI-powered, data-driven decisions that drive innovation in our product portfolio, accelerate insights to action, and delight our customers. The SAP Datasphere Data Marketplace is the cornerstone of how we enable employees to access Data Products more quickly. 

In the 7th episode of the SAP Datasphere Data Marketplace demo series, we demonstrate a comprehensive end-to-end use case that illustrates how SAP implements a data product architecture. The use case is structured into four sequential steps, providing a overview of the entire process.

The Demo begins with finding the right data product in the Data Marketplace, followed by the installation and usage of that product. Next, the focus shifts to the SAP Catalog, demonstrating how data product consumers can benefit from the provided information. The fourth section dives into the modeling process, where existing data products are used to create derived data products for further analysis and reporting. 

Click here to watch the video!

The Demo shows how existing data products are combined and refined to create new, derived data products that enhance analysis and reporting capabilities. The use case is rooted in a CSRD reporting scenario, illustrating how SAP utilizes existing data products and enriches them with additional information to meet specific reporting requirements.

Enjoy watching!

entry.title: Exploring Data Access Control: Ensuring Consistency from SAC to Datasphere and Back - Blog 2
entry.content:
Introduction

Welcome to the next instalment of this blog series. In this post, I will address the question: “Will DAC settings be preserved when sending data from SAC back to Datasphere?” Just to recap, in the previous blog we spoke about the following:

  • What is Data Access Control (DAC) in SAC
  • The different type of DACs – dimension-based and role based
  • Benefits & drawbacks for both dimension-based and role-based controls
  • Which Type of Data Access Control Is Best for Different Situations

 

Throughout this blog, I will discuss the following in relation to data access control coming from SAC to Datasphere:

  • Why would you send data from SAC to Datasphere?
  • Scenario 1 - will DACs be preserved when sending data from SAC back to Datasphere?
  • Scenario 2 - will DACs be preserved when sending data from Datasphere to SAC?

 

Why would you send data from SAC to Datasphere?

Typically, we consider Datasphere as the backend and SAC as the frontend for reporting. However, in this scenario, SAC is used as the backend to store planning data in a planning model. We then aim to send this data from SAC to Datasphere. But why? Here are several reasons:

  • Advanced Data Processing and Transformation - Datasphere provides advanced data transformation capabilities. By sending data from SAC, users can use Datasphere's tools for complex data processing that might not be possible directly within SAC. You can also enrich data with additional information from other sources stored in Datasphere. This can enhance the value, and insights derived from SAC data.

 

  • Enhanced Analytical Capabilities - Datasphere offers advanced analytics tools that can be used to analyse planning data in more depth. This includes predictive analytics and machine learning capabilities that can provide more insightful planning forecasts.

 

  • Scalability and Performance - Datasphere is designed to handle large volumes of data efficiently, ensuring that data can be processed and analysed without performance bottlenecks. Offloading heavy data processing tasks to Datasphere can improve the performance of SAC, ensuring that both planning, and analytics tasks are carried out efficiently.

 

Let’s consider 2 scenarios:

  1. DACs are applied in SAC for User 1 to only see the Soft Drinks data – will user 1 be able to only see the Soft Drinks data when they log into Datasphere?
  2. DACs are applied in Datasphere for User 1 to only see the Soft Drinks data - will user 1 be able to only see the Soft Drinks data when they log into SAC?

 

Scenario 1

In the first scenario, we aim to confirm that DAC settings applied to user 1 within SAC do not carry over when the data from SAC is imported to Datasphere for the same user. Specifically, within the SAC tenant, a manager will assign DAC to user 1, restricting their view to only Soft Drinks data in SAC. Since the DACs are managed within SAC, they will not be enforced in Datasphere when previewing the imported data, as Datasphere does not directly handle the data with these DAC configurations.

 

Steps

  1. Setting up DACs in SAC & setting up the connection
  2. Import the fact data
  3. Result

 

1. Setting up DACs in SAC & setting up the connection

Setting up DAC in SAC has been discussed here in part 1 of this blog series. The detailed steps for setting up a connection between the two tenants can be found here. For a quick overview, refer to the following steps:

  1. Create an OAuth Client in SAC: Make sure to note the relevant information.
  2. Create a CDI Connection to SAC in Datasphere: Use the information noted in the previous step to complete this process.

 

2. Import the fact data

Once the connection is created, I imported the fact data from a remote table in Datasphere from SAC. Once imported, you can add a graphical view on top of this remote table.

 

3. Result

We can then preview the data from this view. To our surprise, my user can see all drink departments. This shows that the DACs that were created in SAC have not followed through to Datasphere. You can see below all drink departments coming through from SAC.

 

 

Scenario 2

In the second scenario, we want to verify that DACs applied within the Datasphere tenant are still enforced when the user accesses the data through SAC. Specifically, when a manager assigns a DAC to user 1, restricting their view to only Soft Drinks data in Datasphere, this restriction should persist when the user accesses the same data in SAC. The SAC story will access the data directly from Datasphere, bypassing any internal SAC models. Therefore, the DACs configured in Datasphere will remain effective, ensuring that user 1 can still only view the Soft Drinks data within SAC.

 

Steps

  1. Setting up DACs in Datasphere & setting up the connection
  2. Import Actual Data from Datasphere via an OData Connection
  3. Result

 

1. Setting up DACs in Datasphere & setting up the connection

Setting up DAC in Datasphere has been discussed here. For this example, In Datasphere, we assigned DACs to user 1 so that they can only view the Soft Drinks data. See data preview below.

 

The detailed steps for setting up a connection between the two tenants can be found here & here. For a quick overview, refer to the following steps:

  1. Create an OAuth Client in Datasphere: Make sure to note the relevant information.
  2. Create an OData Connection to Datasphere in SAC: Use the information noted in the previous step to complete this process.

 

2. Import Actual Data from Datasphere via an OData Connection

We have discussed the steps to import the actual data from Datasphere via an OData connection here. Just to give a quick summary you must first start by creating a new model in the Modeler and select "Start with data." Choose "OData Services" and select the connection you previously created. Create a new query, add the necessary query details, and select the desired table with up to 100 columns. After importing the data, you can clean and finalize your model. Finally, create a story to test and ensure everything is functioning correctly.

 

3. Result

When user 1 accesses the story within SAC, the DACs will maintain its effectiveness. This means that user 1 can only see the data related to the Soft Drinks department. This data is stored in Datasphere, the Data Access Controls (DAC) are, therefore, managed by Datasphere. The story in SAC accesses this data directly from Datasphere, not from an internal SAC model. Therefore, any DAC configured in Datasphere remains effective when the data is viewed through an SAC story.

 

 

See the diagram below to see how the DACs work between the SAC and Datasphere tenants.

 

 

Conclusion

In this final part of our series, we explored whether Data Access Controls (DAC) follow through when sending data from SAC back to Datasphere. We examined the setup of DAC in SAC and discussed the steps for establishing a connection between SAC and Datasphere. Our findings reveal that when data is stored in Datasphere, DACs are managed by Datasphere and remain effective when accessed via SAC. However, if data from an SAC model is imported into Datasphere, the DACs configured in SAC do not apply in Datasphere, as the data is not accessed directly within SAC. Understanding these distinctions is key to managing data effectively across platforms.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in August
entry.content:
Vacation period ⛱️ comes to an end, and we delivered a strong release in August. There are also lots of excellent new blogs in the community and top features on YouTube for below.

 

My top features in August

 

Compatibility Contracts

To encourage confidence in the stability of your views and to guarantee backward compatibility when they are updated, you can now set their Release State to Released. Once a view is released, it must continue to provide the same output columns until it is replaced by a successor, at which point it can be deprecated and, eventually, decommissioned.
The release states are an optional feature. By default, all views are set to Not Released and there is no obligation to release any view.
More information.

 

Workload Management

You can now set space priorities and statement limits for all the spaces in the new Workload Management page under System and Configuration. In the space details pages, you can view the values set for the space priority and statement limits, but you can no longer edit them there. 
Similarly, on the CLI we have deprecated some parameters for the space definition, and they will be removed in future releases. At the same time new ones are introduced and recommended to be used going forward. 
More information.

 

Change Space Upon Import

When working with content packages previously, you were restricted to import them into spaces with the same technical name as the spaces listed in the package. Now you can choose which spaces to import into by selecting a Space Mapping for each space listed in your package, as long as any shared objects can be resolved in the same package.

This helps you to

  • import content on the same tenant into different spaces
  • import a package more than once in the same tenant thus creating copies in different spaces
  • support for intra-tenant transport i.e., space to space transport using the Content Network

More information.

 

 

Support Content on SAP Help

@Ali_Chalhoub already started a few blogs highlighting the new Support Content pages on SAP help for SAP Datasphere. These pages cover troubleshooting and analysis guides, how-to guides, technical details, and more. This is just the beginning, and they will be extended over time.

These blog highlight some topics covered on the pages, and there is already much more:

  • Troubleshooting of Performance Issues Between SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and Datasphere
  • Using SAP Cloud Integration to Consume/Connect to Datasphere OData API with OAuth 2.0
  • Understanding and Documenting the Communication Flow in Remote table, Replication and Dataflow

 

My top blogs in August

@GaetanSaulnier announces the general availability of the technical integration between SAP Datasphere and the Collibra Data Intelligence Platform in his blog and shares a lot of details
➡️ SAP Datasphere and Collibra: End-to-End Data Catalog Integration

@FlorianN and @GregoryRohloff added another blog and video each to the already existing series about the data marketplace:
➡️ SAP Datasphere data marketplace - behind the scenes - new video for marketplace video series
➡️ E2E Use Case: Realizing a Data Product Architecture with SAP IT

@SandyTran started a great blog and video series covering native features in SAP Datasphere which people with strong SAP BW skills will certainly love in her blog
➡️ SAP Datasphere: BW Series – Getting Started.

 

Blogs for our Japanese Community

  • SAP Datasphereの翻訳機能による多言語設定の方法
  • SAP Datasphere ビジネスコンテンツ概要とインストール手順
  • ビジネスコンテンツ「 Finance Foundation for SAP S/4HANA and S/4HANA Cloud」を実装してみよう！
  • SAP Datasphere ビジネスコンテンツ概要とインストール手順
  • SAP Integration Suiteを使ってSAP Datasphereからデータを抽出する方法 OData編

 

More blogs to check out 👇

  • Exploring Data Access Control: Ensuring Consistency from SAC to Datasphere and Back - Blog 2
  • E2E Use Case: Realizing a Data Product Architecture with SAP IT
  • SAP Datasphere Security & Data Access Controls Overview
  • Data Templates for Concur Data Delivery Service (DDS) : POLICY COMPLIANCE SAC Story 
  • Datasphere to Power BI via an ODBC Connection
  • SAP Datasphere and Collibra: End-to-End Data Catalog Integration
  • SAC Report Broadcasting - Blog 1 - Imported Data
  • Data Templates for Concur Data Delivery Service (DDS) : CREDIT CARD PROGRAM MANAGEMENT SAC Story
  • Data Templates for DDS: Expense Administration SAC Story
  • SAP Datasphere data marketplace - behind the scenes - new video for marketplace video series
  • Using SAP Cloud Integration to Consume/Connect to Datasphere OData API with OAuth 2.0
  • Troubleshooting of Performance Issues Between SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and Datasphere
  • Understanding and Documenting the Communication Flow in Remote table, Replication and Dataflow
  • Data Templates for Concur Data Delivery Expense
  • Importing Entities with Semantic of CDS views from S/4HANA On-Premise in SAP Datasphere
  • An Introduction in Automated Machine Learning with FLAML
  • Modernize your BW Landscape: SAP BW/4HANA and SAP Datasphere with SAP BW Bridge
  • SAP Datasphere: BW Series – Getting Started
  • Geo modeling in Datasphere with SAC Geo map
  • Data is the fuel for AI - Modernizing your BW landscape is preparing a data ready enterprise
  • Connect SAP Sales Cloud V2 to SAP Datasphere
  • New Feature - Ability to Disable Welcome Emails
  • E-Learning: SAP Datasphere Lifecycle Management

 

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting and the FAQ for SAP Datasphere.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.

entry.title: Gen AI meets SAP: Improve your sales strategies with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Datasphere and SAC
entry.content:
Introduction

In my previous blog , How Metadata Enrichment can enhance SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine on GenAI scenarios , i focused on leveraging the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine together with Metadata Enrichment through GenAI, to further extend analytical insights. This blog will focus on combining sales data from SAP S/4HANA with product reviews, centrally stored in SAP Datasphere. Leveraging the powerful analytics capabilities of SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), we are will generate actionable sales insights that uplift the conventional sales strategies.

This blog  will explore the benefits of combing sentiment analysis (using SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine and Large Language Models [LLM])  and SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). These cutting-edge technologies together enhance sales and market strategies. These tools, can help businesses transition from basic descriptive analyses to advanced predictive and prescriptive insights, thereby unlocking  significant market opportunities. We'll examine how sentiment analysis reveals customer sentiment from product reviews, while LLMs and SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine facilitate the processing and contextualization of vast data sets. Integration solutions,part of SAP BTP, ensure seamless data flow and scalability, as well as real-time responsiveness. 

Integrating Sales Data with Product Review Sentiments

The first step is to integrate sales data from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere. At the same time, we will take advantage of Generative AI (Gen AI) to analyze product reviews, applying sentiment analysis to understand customer opinions and trends. These reviews, enriched with sentiment scores and categorized against segments of interest (such as product, supplier or delivery experience), are then ingested into SAP Datasphere. This approach harmonizes structured and unstructured data; and also prepares the data for advanced, multi-dimensional analytics.

By combining transactional data with customer sentiments, businesses can gain valuable insights using SAP Analytics Cloud. This integration allows companies to use years of logged data to identify areas for improvement such as enhancing customer support and optimizing delivery services. This data-driven approach helps businesses shift from reactive decision-making to proactive strategies that meet market needs and improve customer satisfaction.

Leveraging Generative AI for Sentiment Analysis of Product Reviews ( Using Synthetic Data )

To conduct this analysis, we used an approach similar to the one in the previous blog. However, in this study, we created synthetic data that closely matches the characteristics of the original dataset. Using synthetic data is important because it allows us to test and train our models without compromising the privacy and integrity of the actual data. Nowadays, generating synthetic data is quite simple with generative AI. In order to do this, each product review was converted into a vector on SAP HANA Cloud within SAP Datasphere. We enriched these vectors with the sentiments and information found in the original dataset.

We used Gen AI to:

  • Create reviews that have the same overall sentiment as the original ones.
  • Mention similar issues encountered, such as product delivery and price sensitivity.
  • Address other relevant factors by considering them at different hierarchical levels.

This approach ensures that our synthetic data is both secure and highly functional for analysis

Synthetic dataset regarding product reviews:

  • User's Overall Review (negative, positive, neutral)
  • Delivery Review (On Time, Delayed, Never Delivered ...) 
  • Price Review (Value for Money, Overpriced ...)
  • Supplier Review (Satisfied, Very Disappointed ...)
  • Customer Support (Satisfied, Very Disappointed ...) 
  • Issues Encountered (Breakouts, Missing Brush ...)
  • Issues Resolutions (Price decrease, Product Refund ...)
  • Resolution Level 1 (Delivery, Packaging ...)
  • Resolution Level 2 (Accessories, Leakage ...)
  • Resolution Level 3 (Color Difference, Expiration Date ...)

    The information concerning the issues of resolutions and their resolving levels was too extensive. An additional pass through using GenAI was necessary to organize this data in a more meaningful manner. Additionally, four more summarized pieces of information regarding the reviews were added :

    • Resolution (Improved Packaging, Improved Pricing ...)
    • Level 1 (Customer Support, Safety Concerns ...)
    • Level 2 (Design Issues, Missing Product ...)
    • Level 3 (Broken Parts, Product Quantity ...)

For example, analyzing two product reviews, the following metadata has been extracted:

 

Supplementary Data Sources and Final Integration

Taking into consideration that this is a demo, we limited the available attributes per "Product", "Customer" and "Supplier" entities. The same constraint apply to the Sales Value and Sales Volume metric. By merging sales data from SAP S/4HANA with sentiment analysis within SAP Datasphere, we developed the final analytical model which will be consumed on SAP Analytics Cloud.

The lineage analysis from the SAP Datasphere model is the following:

This model includes the following dimensions: Product, Calendar, Customer and Supplier along with Sales Data.

Transforming Sales Strategy with SAP Analytics Cloud

By leveraging SAC’s advanced analytical capabilities, businesses can now perform multi-faceted analyses to discover hidden patterns and trends on top of the diversified Data Sources.

Sales teams can drill down into regional customer satisfaction and correlate it with supplier efficiency to identify market-specific strategies. Additionally, predictive analytics powered by SAC can forecast future sales trends by analyzing historical data and enriched generative AI insights, allowing for proactive inventory management and targeted marketing campaigns.Moreover, Reviews outlining customer issues may yield insights for further improvement, e.g. improvement in logistics and delivery services.

The synergy between SAC and Gen AI thus transforms static sales reports into a living strategy dashboard. This powerful combination enables a more informed, responsive, and strategic sales approach, strengthening the business’s competitive position in the market.

Introducing the GenAI-Powered SAP Analytic Cloud Dashboard

    1. Sales Overview

This dashboard offers a comprehensive overview of sales data, which can be utilized by different personas within a business to make well-informed decisions. Here are some of the business scenarios, which highlight how various roles can leverage these graphs.

Scenario 1: Sales Performance Optimization

Persona: Sales manager

Objective: Monitor and enhance sales performance.

Analytical Insights:

  • Total sales value, volume, and products: The sales manager can quickly assess the overall sales performance by looking at the total sales value (260,000.00), total volume (3,871), and total products (1,810). This provides a snapshot of the business health.
  • Sales per category and subcategory: By analyzing the sales per category and subcategory, the manager can identify which product lines are performing well and which are underperforming. For instance, "Ready and Cream" appears to be a significant contributor.
  • Top and bottom performing products: This helps in identifying the best-selling products like "Skin Renew" and the least performing ones like "Skin Prep." The manager can then strategize to promote the bottom products or phase them out.

Scenario 2: Customer Segmentation and Targeting

Persona: Marketing team

Objective: Tailor marketing campaigns based on customer demographics.

Actionable Insights:

  • Average sales per customer age group: The marketing team can use this data to understand which age groups are contributing most to sales. For example, the age group 35-44 has the highest average sales. Gen AI can create personalized marketing messages and campaigns targeting specific age groups based on their purchasing behavior. For instance, if Gen AI identifies that the 35-44 age group prefers "Skin Renew," targeted ads can be created for this demographic.
  • Total Sales and Volume per Year/Month: This helps in identifying seasonal trends and planning marketing campaigns accordingly.

Scenario 3: Supplier Relationship Management

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: Manage and optimize supplier relationships.

Actionable Insights:

  • Total suppliers and average supplier sentiment per category: The supply chain manager can monitor the performance and sentiment of suppliers. For instance, categories like "Bodycare" and "Skincare" have high sentiment scores, indicating good supplier relationships.
  • Sales per product subcategory: This helps in understanding which subcategories are driving sales and ensuring that suppliers for these subcategories are managed effectively. Gen AI can automate communication with suppliers, ensuring timely updates and addressing any issues promptly. This can improve efficiency and maintain strong supplier relationships.

Scenario 4: Product Development and Innovation

Persona: Product development team

Objective: Innovate and develop new products based on market demand.

Actionable Insights:

  • Top and bottom performing products: By analyzing the top-performing products, the product development team can identify features and attributes that are popular among customers.
  • Sales per category and subcategory: This helps in identifying market trends and gaps, guiding the development of new products that meet customer needs.

Scenario 5: Customer Relationship Management

Persona: Customer relationship manager

Objective: Enhance customer satisfaction and loyalty.

Actionable Insights:

  • Total customers and average sales per customer age group: The customer relationship manager can identify key customer segments and tailor engagement strategies accordingly.
  • Sales per product subcategory: Understanding which products are popular among different customer segments can drive personalized customer interactions and recommendations.

    2. Sales / Sentiment Analysis

 

This dashboard provides a detailed overview of sales and sentiment analysis, segmented by various categories, subcategories, suppliers, and time periods. With user-friendly filters and input controls, it allows users to dive deep into the data based on specific categories, subcategories, suppliers, and time frames. Sentiment analysis is visualized through pie charts that represent customer feelings on price, support, delivery, product, and supplier.

Scenario 1: Product Management and Marketing

Persona: Product or marketing manager

Objective: Identify which products are performing well and which are not. For instance, the "Facecare" product in the "Skin Renew" subcategory has the highest sales ($9,195.00) and a high product rating (4.5). This indicates a successful product that could be further promoted.

Scenario 2: Customer Support Improvement

Persona: Customer support manager

Objective: Sentiment analysis identifies areas that need improvement. The "Customer Support Sentiment" pie chart shows that 46.20% of customers have a negative or very negative sentiment towards support. This is a critical area for improvement in support.

Scenario 3: Supplier Relationship Management

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: "Customer Supplier Sentiment" data can be used to evaluate supplier performance. With 52.93% of customers satisfied or very satisfied with suppliers, there is room for improvement.

Scenario 4: Strategic Planning and Forecasting

Persona: Business analyst, strategic planner

Objective: Use the sales trends over time to forecast future sales and plan inventory accordingly. The line graph shows fluctuating sales with peaks around specific dates, indicating potential seasonal trends.

Actionable Insights:

  • Product Rating and Sales: The bar chart shows that "Facecare" and "Shampoo & Conditioner" have high sales and ratings, indicating strong market performance. on the contrary, "Anti-Aging" has lower sales, suggesting a need for marketing efforts or product improvement.
  • Customer Sentiments: The pie charts reveal that while price and delivery sentiments are largely positive, customer support and product sentiments have significant negative feedback. This highlights areas for immediate attention.
  • Product Reviews: The bar chart indicates that "Beauty Tools & Devices" and "Skincare" have the highest volume of reviews, both positive and negative. This suggests these categories are highly engaging but may also have quality issues that need addressing.
  • Customer Demographics: The table shows that the 45-55 age group has the highest sales volume ($75,149.00) and generally positive ratings across all metrics. This demographic could be targeted for loyalty programs or personalized marketing campaigns.

    3. Sales and Sentiment Geo Analysis Overview

This dashboard provides analytical insights, including customer support ratings, price ratings, delivery ratings, supplier ratings, a word cloud of top keywords from product reviews, and a geographical sales distribution map.

Scenario 1: Customer Experience Enhancement

Persona: Customer experience manager

Objective: The customer experience manager can use the dashboard to monitor and improve customer satisfaction.

Actionable Insights:

  • If the delivery rating is low, the manager can investigate logistics and supply chain issues.
  • If the price rating is low, they can consider revising pricing strategies or offering promotions.
  • By analyzing the customer support rating, price rating, delivery rating, and supplier rating, they can identify areas needing improvement.

Scenario 2. Market Expansion Strategy

Persona: Marketing manager

Objective: Use the geographical sales distribution map to identify regions with high and low sales performance.

Actionable Insights:

  • If sales are high in Northern Europe but low in Southern Europe, the manager can allocate more marketing resources to Southern Europe and tailor campaigns to local tastes.
  • Use the word cloud to identify popular product features and emphasize them in marketing materials.

Scenario 3: Product Development and Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Use the word cloud and customer ratings to gather feedback on product performance and identify areas for improvement.

Actionable Insights:

  • If customers frequently mention "razor" and "blade" in a positive context, the manager can consider expanding the product line to include more shaving products.
  • If "smell" and "oil" are mentioned negatively, they can work on improving the product formulation.
  • if "skin" and "hair" are frequently mentioned, the manager can focus on these aspects in product development.

Scenario 4. Supply Chain Optimization

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: Use the delivery rating and geographical sales data to optimize logistics and supply chain operations.

Actionable Insights:

  • If delivery ratings are low in certain regions, the manager can investigate and address bottlenecks in the supply chain.
  • Use sales data to ensure that high-demand regions are adequately stocked.

    4. Detailed Analysis on Issues

Users can explore the complex relationships between product reviews and their corresponding resolution outcomes, segmented by resolution levels ranging from 1 to 3. This tab offers a thorough analysis of how effectively customer issues and reviews have been addressed, providing a detailed view of resolution efficacy. By filtering data based on specific suppliers or products, users can identify patterns and correlations that may affect customer satisfaction and product performance.

Scenario 1: Supplier Performance Analysis

Persona: Supply chain manager

Objective: Identify and address supplier-related issues to improve overall supply chain efficiency.

Actionable Insights:

  • Bar Chart Analysis: The "Nbr of Resolution per Supplier (Top 10)" chart shows that "GlamGlow Goods Delivery" has the highest number of resolutions (287), followed by "PoshParcel" (284) and "BeautyBeam Carriers" (278). This indicates that these suppliers are frequently involved in issues that require resolution.
  • Flow Diagram Analysis: By examining the flow diagram, the Supply Chain Manager can trace specific issues back to these suppliers. For instance, if "GlamGlow Goods Delivery" is frequently associated with "Packaging Problems" or "Delivery Delays," these insights can be used to negotiate better terms or seek alternative suppliers.

Scenario 2: Product Quality Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Enhance product quality and customer satisfaction by addressing common product-related issues.

Actionable Insights:

  • Bar Chart Analysis: The "Nbr of Resolution per Product (Top 10)" chart highlights that "Skin Revive" and "Hair Revive" have the highest number of resolutions (48 and 41 respectively). This suggests that these products are frequently problematic.
  • Flow Diagram Analysis: The Product Manager can use the flow diagram to identify specific issues associated with these products. For example, "Skin Revive" might be linked to "Product Quality Issues" or "Application Difficulties."

Scenario 3: Customer Support Optimization

Persona: Customer support manager

Objective: Streamline customer support processes and improve resolution times.

Actionable Insights:

  • Bar Chart Analysis: The charts provide insights into which products and suppliers are generating the most issues, allowing the Customer Support Manager to allocate resources more effectively.
  • Flow Diagram Analysis: The flow diagram helps in understanding the complexity and interdependencies of various issues. For example, issues like "Instructions Clarity" and "Packaging Problems" might be common across multiple products and suppliers. GENAI can assist in automating responses to common issues, reducing the workload on customer support teams. Furthermore, by analyzing customer feedback, GENAI can provide insights into customer sentiment, helping the support team to prioritize issues that have the most significant impact on customer satisfaction.

    5. Interacting with GPT on Vectorised Documents

Various personas can utilize this interactive widget on the SAC dashboard to pose specific queries. These queries are converted into vector format, matched against Vectorised customer reviews stored in the SAP HANA database, and finally summarized by GPT. This approach facilitates a comprehensive understanding of customer sentiments, performance optimization, campaign effectiveness, product-specific feedback, and competitive analysis, ensuring data-driven strategies across various business functions.

Scenario 1: Customer Sentiment Analysis for Product Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Understand customer feedback and sentiment for product enhancement.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The interactive widget in the fifth tab of the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard allows the Product Manager to pose open-ended questions such as "Which is the best cream for young men?" Using GenAI, this query is translated into a vector format and matched against stored, vectorized customer reviews in the SAP HANA database. The result is a selection of relevant reviews and a summary generated by GPT, providing clear insights into customer preferences and sentiments. This enables the Product Manager to identify key areas for product improvement and innovation based on real customer feedback.

Scenario 2: Sales Performance Optimization

Persona: Sales manager

Objective: Optimize sales strategies and improve product performance.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The sales manager can use the interactive widget to ask questions like "What are the common complaints about our top-selling products?" The Gen AI powered widget translates this query into a vector format and retrieves relevant customer reviews from the SAP HANA database. The GPT-generated summary provides a concise overview of common issues, allowing the sales manager to address these concerns proactively. This insight helps in refining sales strategies and improving customer satisfaction.

Scenario 3: Marketing Campaign Effectiveness

Persona: Marketing professional

Objective: Evaluate the effectiveness of marketing campaigns and understand customer sentiment.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive widget analysis: The Marketing Professional can utilize the interactive widget to ask questions such as "How do customers feel about our latest marketing campaign?" The Gen AI integration translates this query into a vector format and matches it with vectorized customer reviews in the SAP HANA database. The resulting summary generated by GPT provides a clear picture of customer sentiment towards the campaign. This insight helps the Marketing Professional to assess the campaign's impact and make data-driven decisions for future marketing strategies.

Scenario 4: Product-Specific Feedback Analysis

Persona: Quality assurance manager

Objective: Gather detailed feedback on specific products for quality improvement.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The Quality assurance manager can use the interactive widget to ask specific questions like "What are the most common issues with our new skincare line?" The Gen AI powered widget translates this query into a vector format and retrieves relevant customer reviews from the SAP HANA database. The GPT-generated summary highlights the most common issues, providing the quality assurance manager with actionable insights to improve product quality and address customer concerns effectively.

Scenario 5: Competitive Analysis

Persona: Business analyst

Objective: Analyze competitor products and customer reviews for strategic planning.

Actionable Insights:

  • Interactive Widget Analysis: The business analyst can leverage the interactive widget to ask questions such as "How do our products compare to our competitors' in terms of customer satisfaction?" The Gen AI integration translates this query into a vector format and matches it with vectorized customer reviews from both the company's and competitors' products stored in the SAP HANA database. The GPT-generated summary provides a comparative analysis of customer satisfaction, helping the Business Analyst to identify strengths and weaknesses and develop strategic plans to gain a competitive edge.

    6. Customer 360

This tab showcases an Customer 360 Dashboard, integrating GenAI and analytics to provide a comprehensive view of customer interactions and sentiments. This powerful combination enables businesses to extract actionable insights, anticipate customer needs, and customize personalized experiences, driving increased customer satisfaction and loyalty.

Scenario 1: Customer Sentiment Analysis for Product Improvement

Persona: Product manager

Objective: Understand customer feedback and sentiment for product enhancement.

Scenario 2: Proactive Customer Support

Persona: Customer support agent

Objective: Provide personalized and informed customer support. The support agent uses the sentiment analysis and issue resolution data to reassure the customer about the price adjustment and highlight the product's value for money. For instance, if a customer contacts support with a query about the "BlendMaster" product, the agent can leverage the positive sentiment towards product quality and the resolved price issue to build trust and satisfaction.

Scenario 3: Targeted Marketing Campaigns

Persona: Marketing manager

Objective: Design effective marketing campaigns based on customer sentiment. Using the sentiment data, the marketing team identifies that customers appreciate the "TrimPro" product's reliability and wet trimming experience. The campaign can focus on these features to attract similar customer segments, leading to higher engagement and conversion rates.

Technical Coffee Corner

Let's explore the underlying architecture that empowers this sales strategy ecosystem. At the heart of this setup is the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine, which excels in handling high-dimensional vector data by creating a rich vector space representation for unstructured data like product reviews. When unified with the SAP Datasphere, this sophisticated engine facilitates efficient data ingestion, transformation, and harmonization, bridging the gap between isolated SAP S/4HANA transactional data and sentiment-driven insights. To enhance this further, SAP BTP plays a pivotal role in orchestrating these advanced analytics workflows, ensuring seamless data integration, real-time processing, and scalability. Leveraging the power of Large Language Models (LLM), the system employs fine-tuned AI models to contextualize and enrich product reviews, breaking them down into granular sentiment scores and categorized themes. These enriched insights are then fed into SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), where the robust analytics capabilities and interactive dashboards take center stage. SAC's connectivity and integration layers enable smooth data transfers, while its advanced algorithms enable predictive and prescriptive analytics, providing a multi-dimensional view of sales metrics. The cooperation of these platforms not only elevates the data processing capabilities but also equips businesses to generate forward-looking insights. For any help or clarification, you may contact us at @DKK  , @Dimitrios_Lyras .

Conclusion

Integrating Gen AI with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Datasphere, and SAP Analytics Cloud offers substantial benefits for both IT and business personas. For IT professionals, this combination simplifies the management of complex data landscapes, ensuring robust data security while enhancing the scalability of analytics tasks. With advanced analytical models in SAP Analytics Cloud, IT teams can more easily manage multiple data sources, thereby improving operational efficiency. Business users—including sales managers, marketing teams, product developers, and customer support agents—gain critical insights that drive strategic decision-making. The deep sentiment analysis enhances personalized marketing campaigns, improves customer support, and optimizes supply chain interactions. Utilizing these insights helps organizations shift from reactive to proactive strategies, significantly boosting sales performance, enhancing customer satisfaction, and increasing overall market responsiveness. This comprehensive approach ensures that both IT and business stakeholders can achieve their goals more effectively, leading to sustained business growth and a decisive competitive advantage.

entry.title: Integrating SAP S/4HANA Data into SAP HANA Cloud Using SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
As businesses strive to make data-driven decisions, integrating data across different systems becomes crucial. Below we describe our approach of replicating SAP S/4HANA data into SAP HANA Cloud using SAP Datasphere. This process ensures that your business data is always up-to-date and readily available in SAP HANA Cloud, enabling you to gain critical insights and leverage advanced analytics using powerful SAP HANA Cloud engines. 

Overview of the Integration Process 

 

 

The integration starts with extracting data from the ABAP Core Data Services (CDS) layer in your SAP S/4HANA system. CDS views in SAP S/4HANA provide a structured and efficient way to access and expose data, which forms the foundation of this data replication process. The access through the ABAP CDS layer and the ABAP Pipeline Engine absolutely ensures the semantical integrity of the replicated SAP S/4HANA business data.

A connection is then established between SAP Datasphere and your SAP S/4HANA system. If your SAP S/4HANA system is on-premises or a private cloud edition, the SAP Cloud Connector is necessary to facilitate secure communication. However, for the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, this connector isn't required. 

Within SAP Datasphere, you configure a Replication Flow that defines how data is extracted from SAP S/4HANA and replicated to SAP HANA Cloud. This flow includes specifying the source objects (the CDS views), the target system (SAP HANA Cloud), and the structure in which data will be stored. Moreover, the Replication Flow offers flexibility in data transfer modes, allowing for various options such as initial full loads, delta loads, or scheduled periodic loads. 

SAP Datasphere also offers monitoring capabilities, enabling you to track the progress of the replication process and ensure that data is flowing as expected. Any necessary transformations, such as data projection and mapping, can be applied within the flow to align the data with the target structure in SAP HANA Cloud. 

Once the data is extracted and transformed, it is securely loaded into the predefined target tables within your SAP HANA Cloud instance, ready for further processing and analysis. 

 

Additional Resources 

As this functionality have been available since some time, now we added two key deliverables: the mission and the booster, which further simplify implementation of such a replication scenario. 

The Mission offers a step-by-step guide to setting up the necessary infrastructure and configuring the required settings across SAP S/4HANA, SAP HANA Cloud, and SAP Datasphere. The mission "Integrate SAP S/4HANA Data into SAP HANA Cloud leveraging SAP Datasphere" could be found on SAP  Discovery Center here: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4418. 

 

 

In the mission there is a project board containing details about how to complete every step needed for starting data replication. 

 

 

The Booster deploys SAP Datasphere in your account by following a guided step-by-step approach. The booster could be found at your BTP Cockpit global account: <global account url>/boosters. 

 

 

With the help of the Mission and the Booster, you can start replicating SAP S/4HANA data into SAP HANA Cloud using SAP Datasphere even faster. 

Additional References

  • Replication Flow Blog Series Part 1 – Overview | SAP Blogs 
  • Replication Flow Blog Series Part 2 – Premium Outbound Integration | SAP Blogs 
  • Replication Flow Blog Series Part 3 – Integration with Kafka 
  • Replication Flow Blog Series Part 4 – Sizing 
  • Replication Flow Blog Series Part 5 – Integration between SAP Datasphere and Databricks  
  • Replication Flow Blog Series Part 6 – Confluent as a Replication Target 
  • Getting Started with SAP Datasphere 
  • Creating Users and Spaces and Preparing Connectivity 
  • Configure the Size of Your SAP Datasphere Tenant 
  • Creating a Replication Flow  
  • Get started with SAP HANA Cloud 
  • SAP HANA Cloud Documentation 
  • SAP HANA Cloud Getting Started Guide 
  • SAP HANA Cloud Capacity Unit Estimator

entry.title: How to Connect S4HANA Public Cloud With SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Author Biography: 

My name is Saikat Pal & I am a Manager in Data & Analytics practice of PwC India over 14+ years of experience in SAP Datasphere, SAP BTP HANA Cloud, SAP BTP CAPM, SAP BW4HANA, S4 HANA Embedded Analytics with CDS technology. I have exposure in techno-functional consulting in Material Management, Purchasing, Sales & Distribution, Customer Relationship  Management ( CRM ), Supply Chain Management ( SCM ) from Steel Making Industry, Pharma, Petrochemical, Cement, Solar etc.

Introduction:

After reading this article you will be able to establish a connection between SAP S4HANA Public Cloud with SAP Datasphere Public Cloud

Benefits:

      You will be able to replicate data from S4HANA Public Cloud to SAP Datasphere Public Cloud

Pre-requisites:

S4HANA Public Cloud user should have access to Maintain Communication Users, Communication Systems,Communication Arrangements app.

Steps for Configuration:
     

  • Log In to S4 HANA Public Cloud Tenant. Go to My Space

      

  • Search and select the “Maintain Communication Users” app​ from S4HANA Public Cloud

  • "Maintain Communication Users " app will open in S4HANA Public Cloud. Then click on New Button to create New User.

  • Put the User Name “YY1_USR_DS”

  • Put the User Name, Description, and Password as shown below and click “Create”​

  • Once Communication User is created, a CC* User ID will be generated​

  • Search and select the “Communication Systems” app and click on “New”​ to create new Communication System.

  • Give the System ID created earlier and the Host Name as "DWC"

  • Scroll down further and add User for Inbound Connection:​

  • Search the user that you have created earlier

  • Search and select the “Communication Arrangements” app and click “New”​

  • A pop-up box will appear with the following details

  • In the scenario, search for SAP_COMM_0532 and select it. Once selected give the Communication System which was created​

  • Once the Communication System is selected, scroll down for Authorization Group ID and select SAP_DPS_ALL​

  • Scroll down and identify the Inbound Communication and the Inbound Services. Review your inputs and click save.​

  • Now Log in to DataspherePlatform and click on Space Management -> Connections -> Go To Connections​

  • Select the Business Name and edit. Give the User Name and Password of Communication User and save​

  • Select the Business Name and validate. Please note that remote table was disabled as Data Provisioning Agent(DP Agent) was not installed at that time.

  • Now click on Data Builder -> New Data Flow​

 

 

  • Click on Sources from the Repository-Source option & you will be able to see S4HANA Cloud conneciton that you have created

 

Note: In this article Data Flow scenario is considered. You can also select Communication scenario SAP_COMM_0531 to replicate data via remote table.

entry.title: Streamlining Data Sharing: Emailing CSV Files from SAP Datasphere (DWC) using DIC
entry.content:
Introduction:

In today's data-driven world, extracting data from various sources and sharing it with stakeholders is a common requirement. Recently I had to work on a requirement that required me to extract a Datasphere table and send the extract as an attachment in an email through DIC. In this blog post, let’s explore how to extract a table from SAP Datasphere, convert it to a CSV file, and send it via email using the Send Email operator in SAP Data Intelligence Cloud. Let's dive in

Connection Setup:

Configure the SMTP connection in SAP Data Intelligence Cloud. This connection type required the following parameters: Host, Port, User Authentication, and Password of the SMTP connection.

Make sure you set up the connection properly to enable email sending functionality.

Creating the Graph:

To accomplish this task, Let us create graph consisting of the following components: Table Consumer, Python3 operator, Send Email operator, Wiretap (optional), and Graph Terminator.

  1. Table Consumer Operator: The table Consumer operator is configured to select the desired table from SAP Datasphere. This operator will fetch the required table for further processing.
  2. Python3 Operator: The output from the Table Consumer operator had to be converted into a message format. I used the 'Table to Message Converter' operator to achieve this conversion and connect its output to the input port of the Python3 operator.                                                                          Configure the input and output ports of the Python3 operator to handle messages.
  3. Python Code: Inside the Python3 operator, the following code snippet is used to convert the message into a CSV file and attach it to an email.
  4. Send Email Operator: Send Email operator is configured with the SMTP connection details. The output port of the Python3 operator was connected to the Send Email operator. This will pass the generated CSV file as an attachment to the email.
  5. Wiretap Operator (Optional): If you want to monitor or capture intermediate results, you can add a Wiretap operator. Connect the Send Email operator to the Wiretap operator and then to the Graph Terminator.

Executing the Graph: After configuring and connecting all the operators, save the graph and execute it. This will initiate the process of extracting the table, converting it to a CSV file, and sending it as an email attachment.

Conclusion: In this blog post, we learned how to leverage the power of SAP Data Intelligence Cloud to extract data from SAP Datasphere, convert it to a CSV file using Python, and send it via email using the Send Email operator. This functionality provides a seamless way to share data insights with stakeholders, enabling effective collaboration and decision-making. It's important to note that the provided Python code snippet is a basic example and may require modifications to suit specific use cases and environments.

entry.title: Building a business data fabric: SAP Datasphere vs. DIY implementations
entry.content:
Providing your teams with relevant, real-time analysis enables them to make more informed and agile business decisions. But we often hear from customers that scattered information and siloed data prevent their employees from getting the rapid insights they need.

That’s why so many enterprises are looking to revolutionize data management by building a business data fabric. This is a data management architecture that focuses on delivering an integrated, semantically-rich data layer over underlying data landscapes to provide seamless and scalable access to data without duplication. But when it comes to building a business data fabric, what is the best approach?

Putting SAP Datasphere to the test

SAP Datasphere, a comprehensive data service built on SAP Business Technology Platform, is the foundation for a business data fabric. It enables organizations to deliver meaningful data to every data consumer—with business context and logic intact. It provides capabilities from data ingestion through self-service data access across SAP and non-SAP data.  

In its Data Fabric Field Test: SAP vs. DIY, GigaOm measured the ease of use, time, effort, and total cost of deploying SAP Datasphere as compared to a do-it-yourself (DIY) approach to building a data fabric with a blend of other applications. The DIY implementation path included solutions for data warehousing and data integration, along with a data catalog and data lake. The results are very compelling:  

Ease of use

GigaOm evaluated the ease of use when it comes to carrying out a variety of data management tasks. It found that, compared to the DIY approach, SAP Datasphere was on average 23.1% easier to use, with data modeling 72.4% easier and data security 78.6% easier.

Time

The report found that deploying SAP Datasphere took significantly less time than building the DIY implementation path. In fact, the initial build timeline for SAP Datasphere was about 2.5 times shorter.

Effort

To quantify effort, GigaOm evaluated the amount of effort required for enterprises to complete the three main stages of establishing a data fabric, which they defined as:

  1. Initial build/migration
  2. Continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) of new features and enhancements
  3. Administration (including maintenance and user support)

For all three stages, the report found that deploying SAP Datasphere requires less effort than the DIY approach. And for the CI/CD and maintenance stages, GigaOm found that it takes about a third to a half of the headcount to run SAP Datasphere as compared to the DIY implementation path.

Total cost

Across all data fabric sizes, the infrastructure costs to support SAP Datasphere were about 69% lower than those of the DIY approach. The report also found that SAP Datasphere provided a savings of 63% on initial build costs, 53% on CI/CD costs, and 41% on administration costs. What’s more, the solution reduced total cost of ownership by 138% over a three-year period.


Find out more

In my next two blogs, I’ll highlight the ROI and deployment best practices for SAP Datasphere. In the meantime, read the GigaOm Data Fabric Field Test: SAP vs. DIY study to learn more.

entry.title: Currency conversion in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Currency conversion is a fundamental capability across most SAP products, playing a vital role in enabling accurate financial reporting and analysis across different currencies. Whether you're working within the traditional BW environment or leveraging the modern features of SAP Analytics Cloud, there are various techniques available to handle currency conversion effectively.

In this blog, I’ll take you through a comprehensive, step-by-step guide to modeling currency conversion in SAP Datasphere, helping you unlock the full potential of this essential feature.

Disclaimer: All the data created are for demo purposes, the approach is used to demo the functionality of the currency conversion in SAP Datasphere, not intended for production design or usage.

Step overview:

1. Create SAP currency tables/views

2. Create a task chain to trigger data loading for SAP currency exchange rate/currency code/decimals, etc

3. Create a transaction data table and view on top of it

4. Create a currency conversion column to perform currency conversion

Detailed steps:

1. Create SAP currency tables/views

From Data Builder, create SAP currency tables/views, we can load data from S4 source system(connection needs to be set up beforehand) 

The below screenshot shows the source of currency tables/views, multiple data flows also be created when currency conversion views are added to the space, you can also choose to get the source manually.

 

2. Create a task chain to combine all the data flows to trigger data loading for SAP currency exchange rate/currency code/decimals, etc 

Running the task chain to load all the currency related data from S4

 

3. Create a transaction data table and view on top of it

When creating a transaction data table, we will need to assign 'Currency code' semantic type to the Currency column

In this demo, we are going to convert transaction currency to

1. Global currency

2. Currency from Input Parameter

Let's first take a look at the transaction data we created:

Exchange rate we loaded from S4 source system, in order to derive the correct date, we will need to use 99999999 minus the 'Date as of which the Exchange rate is Effective' 

4. Create a currency conversion column to perform currency conversion

  • Convert to GlobalCurrency

In the view build, select add a 'Currency Conversion Column', a new column will be generated automatically, configure the 'Source Amoutn Column', 'Source Currency', and 'Target Currency', in this case, it's 'GCCurrency', we will use the CURRENT_DATE() as a reference date to perform the conversion,

Note: you can also create your own formula to use different date as reference date

Error handling configuration is also available to cater for failed conversion

  • Convert to Target Currency based on Input Parameter

In this case, we need to create Input Parameter from the downstream target, so that it will appear when we create currency conversion column in the calcuation node

Input parameter created in downstream target:

We go back to calculation node of currency conversion column, we can select Input parameter as a new option

Save and deploy the view

Final output

Now if we preview the final output, it will prompt us to choose the target currency

The value help populate all the currency code nicely

 

We choose JPY in this case, you can see the conversion performed nicely with result in both global currency(USD) and JPY: 

Conclusion

The currency conversion modeling in SAP Datasphere is very straightforward, it can support multiple currency codes easily. 

Hope you enjoy the reading, feel free to leave your comment or question below. 

SAP Datasphere BW (SAP Business Warehouse)

entry.title: SAP Datasphere - Remote Table dynamic filter pushdown
entry.content:
Problem description

When integrating data via Remote Table in SAP Datasphere, filtering is limited. The limitations depend on the complexity of the SQL the DPAgent is able to push down to the source. An often seen requirement is to add a filter where the data should be filtered dynamically. This Blog shows a workaround to achieve a dynamic filtering.

For this use case, we use the ABAP Table MSEG which is not delta enabled. The MSEG table contains information about materials whenever a material movement occurs. We want to determine all materials that have a "Best-Before date" which is exactly 200 days in the future. For that, we want to add a dynamic filter on the"Best-Before date" field, which is called "VFDAT". It is being loaded into Datasphere as a String(8) in the format YYYYMMDD.

Inside the remote table itself we can only define a hard filter on a specific date. What we want instead is a dynamic filter so the integrated data changes as time goes on.

1st approach

The easiest approach is to build a view on top of the unpersisted remote table. The SQL can look like this:

 

 

select "MANDT","MBLNR","MJAHR","VFDAT" from QS4_MSEG where "VFDAT" = ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, 200)

 

 

The result set consists of 3 records. But when looking at the Remote Query of this View, the WHERE clause is not being sent to the source. The we get 3 records in the end but there are 34,516 records moving between the source system and Datasphere. So Datasphere queries the entire dataset and then filters afterwards to show the correct result because it is unable to push the WHERE clause to the source, as it is too complex.

When working with huge amounts of data, we want to limit the amount of data moving between the systems.
For that, we need to find a way to push the WHERE clause to the source.

2nd approach

We can do so by declaring variables in an SQL Script view. The SQL Script can look like this:

 

 

DECLARE myDate DATE = ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, 200);
DECLARE myDateString NVARCHAR(8) = TO_NVARCHAR(myDate, 'YYYYMMDD');
return select "MANDT","MBLNR","MJAHR","VFDAT" from QS4_MSEG where "VFDAT" = myDateString;

 

 

Instead of trying to push the complex WHERE clause, this script resolves the WHERE clause first when declaring the variable. The result set consist of the same 3 records as before, but when looking at the remote query, we can see that there WHERE clause is being pushed. It cannot visualise the WHERE clause in the remote query, so a "?" is being displayed but we can see in that only 3 records are being pulled.

Conclusion

It is apparent that the query from approach 2 is faster and leaner than the query from approach 1. It is possible that the data volume requires the leanness and pace of approach 2. This seems like a valid workaround for all use cases that depend on a dynamic filtering when integrating data via remote table.

entry.title: Four questions to ask when adding CDC, CDP and Emarsys to your marketing toolset
entry.content:
Coming off another disappointing loss to the Kansas City Chiefs in the NFL Super Bowl LVIII earlier this year, San Francisco 49ers football head coach Kyle Shanahan offered his perspective on the team’s mentality going into the offseason:

You look at (every option) every single year and you try to become the best team possible with the situation that you're in. You don't just say, 'Hey, we're upgrading here this year.' You've got to make sure that's available. And if it's not, then you get stronger in a different area. There's lots of ways you can win. You just have to keep trying to find that way.

This is a puzzle that teams across all professional sports leagues are trying to solve as they return from an offseason of free agent signings, trades and transactions. How will new personnel fit into our organization and how do we optimize their strengths? What kind of process and platform changes will new leadership and their processes bring? How do these changes move the needle and bring us closer to our season goals?

One well-known NHL sports team is asking these questions not only about their team but also recent changes to their marketing technology.

Over the past three summers, this customer took steps to optimize their business’ approach to customer marketing with the addition of three new SAP systems–Customer Data Cloud (CDC), Customer Data Platform (CDP) and Emarsys–and have been monitoring performance of select email campaigns ever since. These solutions replaced a longtime player in their CX architecture–SAP Marketing Cloud (SMC)–which the customer had been using for years create segments of users across multiple sources and market to them with personalized email communications.

A system overhaul like this requires not only business investment in the new technology, but also team training, solution alignment and periodic assessment. From discovery through launch, the customer kept in mind the following questions–ones that any company should consider when planning a shift from a single platform to a multi-cloud solution.

What value do we get from the trade?

While SMC helped the team with email marketing needs, members were never able to take full advantage of the platform due, in part, to platform limitations. With the SAP acquisition of CDC (formerly “Gigya”) in late 2017 and Emarsys in 2020, along with the release of CDP in 2020, the customer saw an opportunity to improve their marketing solution and leverage strengths from each platform:

  • CDC – for capturing user marketing consent, newsletter opt-ins and customer data through Screen-Sets and Hosted Pages.
  • CDP – for handling complex merging, segmentation and orchestration with systems based on data privacy permissions.
  • Emarsys – for marketing automation, improved workflows and customer experience.

How do new players fit within the current system?

For the initial two program phases, the customer focused on replacing SMC with a core CX framework that leverages CDC and DSP as data sources, paving the way for future integrations. 

CX Marketing Architecture including CDC, CDP and Emarsys

 By shifting from SMC to a multi-cloud solution, the customer can get the most value out of each system while not being restricted to just one platform. The flexibility and agility gained through this approach will be key as the customer looks to connect to other data sources, both from SAP and outside of SAP.

How well does each player know their role?

With multiple solutions, however, comes a new question around overlapping features and functions.

Just like in any team with talented players, there are bound to be some systems with overlapping capabilities. There isn’t one solution that fits all, so it’s important to invest time to clearly define the roles of each application and decide which platform is best suited for handling day-to-day tasks.

Take, for example, segmentation. Both CDP and Emarsys platforms have tools to segment users, so which system should customers use to build the segment?

For this customer, the direction largely depended on answers to a few questions:

  • What are the business use cases / campaigns that we are looking to launch in this phase?
  • What data needs to be available in each system to carry out the campaigns?
  • What system would be easier for a non-technical marketing team to use and maintain?

In the end, the solution that the customer landed on involved a combination of the two platforms:

  • Emarsys for non-complex segmentation needs (e.g. subscribed to a newsletter or are part of a specific tier). This requires that any permanent, re-usable contact information from CDP is synced with Emarsys.
  • CDP for more advanced field calculations and segmentation (e.g. users who purchased tickets to X game) and send trigger events to Emarsys when email (e.g. pre-event, post-event) should be sent to the user.

How are we going to develop our players and team?

The goal for the development staffing for any professional sports team is to try and turn players into serviceable assets. The same can be said for new marketing technologies.

The customer is already looking forward to the next integrations that require more advanced capabilities of CDC, CDP and Emarsys and expand the dataset coming from DSP. With a myriad of other data sources available in their network, from parking reservations and restaurant purchases to storefront and arena (Wi-Fi) logins, there are many ways to further develop the solution and personalize customer communication, which in turn breeds loyal customers.

It won’t happen overnight, of course. Developing a multi-cloud marketing solution requires thoughtful planning, iterative updates, and commitment. While this customer’s off-season marketing technology changes marked a big step in the right direction, there is still plenty of work ahead to realize their full marketing vision.

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Machine Learning with SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere is a comprehensive data orchestration platform, with a graphical user interface that also addresses less technical business users. SAP HANA Cloud is embedded into SAP Datasphere, which gives SAP Datasphere access to its multi-model capabilities, including the built-in Machine Learning.

This blog shows how to implement a SAP Business AI project with a time-series forecast by using the embedded Predictive Analysis Library. Trigger a forecast from your favourite Python environment without having to extract the data from SAP HANA Cloud, thanks to our Python package hana_ml. Avoid data duplication (keeping your architecture lean) and benefit from the in-memory power.

The whole code of this project can be downloaded from this repository.

Should you prefer to work with a native / stand-alone version of SAP HANA Cloud, then you may prefer to follow this blog  instead.

Table of contents

  • Use case
  • Architecture / prerequisites
  • SAP Datasphere
  • Python / Jupyter Notebooks
  • Time-series forecasting
    • Data upload
    • Forecasting a time-series
    • Forecasting multiple time-series
  • Sharing forecasts with SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Going further

 This blog was put together by @AndreasForster and @YannickSchaper 

 

Use case

We have chosen to go through a time-series forecasting example, as this is an extremely common requirement for the most diverse projects. Many standard applications from SAP already have time-series forecasting built in, such as SAP Analytics Cloud or SAP Integrated Business Planning. If you have a requirement, that goes beyond a standard application, you can leverage the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), ie SAP Datasphere and its embedded SAP HANA Cloud. Alternatively you can also work with a stand-alone SAP HANA Cloud instead, then please see this blog. 

In our case now we would like to forecast how many nights foreign tourists or business travellers will spend in Switzerland in the coming months. We are interested in the total numbers, but we also want to know how many nights different nationalities will spend in the country. 

Such forecasts of overnight stays can be useful for many purposes, for instance to help decide where to spend a Marketing budget. It can also help the hotel industry to have the right number of staff (and languages) working through the year.

These might just be examples we invented ourselves, based on data that is publicly available (thanks to the opendata.swiss portal!), but such time-series forecasts are used extremely often in the business world. We have used time-series forecasting in many diverse projects, from forecasting the Net Working Capital for Treasury departments to solar power production at a utilities company. 

All these use cases have in common, that in such a BTP project an expert (you!) creates a forecast that is then shared with the business users, who might not want to know all technical details of how the forecasts were created.

 

Architecture / prerequisites

To follow this blog's instructions, you need to have access to:

  • A productive instance of SAP Datasphere. Note that the free-tier or free-trial options of SAP Datasphere do not suffice, since these have only 2 virtual CPUs. The embedded Machine however requires the Script Server to be activated, which requires 3 virtual CPUs.
  • A Python environment. We explain how to set up miniconda, but if you already have an existing Python installation, you should be able to use this as well. Of course, you could also use the Business Application Studio on our Business Technology Platform. 
  • It helps if you have a bit of Python or scripting experience. But we hope this blog can also be a good entry point for someone who is just starting out with Python.
  • Motivation to get to know SAP HANA's Predictive Analysis Library. 

The overall architecture for this implementation is centered around SAP Datasphere.

Currently the Predictive Analysis Library provides all these algorithms, and the list keeps growing. 

 

SAP Datasphere

Let's configure SAP Datasphere, so that we can use the embedded Machine Learning.

Step 1: Activate the Script server

The script server must be activated in the SAP Datasphere's Tenant Configuration, please see this SAP Note. This should only take a few seconds to take effect. The free version of SAP Datasphere do not have sufficient virtual CPUs to activate the Script Server. Any productive system however does have a minimum of 3 vCPUs and can therefore use the Machine Learning.

Step 2: Create a Database User

The Machine Learning is triggered by a Space's Database User, hence create a Database User in the Space Management (our space is called AISPACE).

 You need to tick the options

  • "Enable Automated Predictive Library (APL) and Predictive Analysis Library (PAL)": This option only shows up, if the Script Sever is enabled.
  • "Enable Read Access (SQL)": To allow the Database User to access the data.
  • "Enable Write Access (SQL, DDL, & DML)": Required as many algorithms need to write temporary data to the system, and also to allow the Database User to write further data into the space (ie predictions).

Do not forget to click "Deploy" after the user was created. Only after the deployment you can open the new users info dialog (little "i" symbol on the right) from where you get the logon credentials. Note down the

  • host name
  • full Database User Name, ie "AISPACE#AIUSER"
  • password 

Make sure to keep the password somewhere safe. If you forget the passwird, you cannot get it back. You would need to request a new password instead.

Step 3: Allow IP address

Your Python environment's external IP address must be added to SAP Datasphere's allow list. Otherwise your attempt to connect from Python will be denied. In the System Configuration go to the "IP Allowlist" and add your external IP address. As a quick test you can consider allow all IP addresses by adding the range "0.0.0.0/0" to the allow list. Don't forget to remove this later though. Further down we show how to identify your exact external IP address. Adding this to the allowlist would obviously be more secure.

It might take a few seconds for any changes to this list to take effect. 

 

Python / Jupyter Notebooks

Use a Python environment of your choice to be able to use Jupyter Notebooks. There are many options, ie with SAP (Business Application Studio) or non-SAP environments. The following steps show how to run Jupyter Notebooks on your local laptop by using the free environment miniconda.

Download and install miniconda. The easiest option is probably the graphical installer.

Open the "Anaconda Prompt" that was installed by minconda. On Windows just use the Operating System's "Search" box to find the prompt.

miniconda allows you to have multiple Python environments side by side. Making changes in one of these environments does not affect the others. This can be very useful to run tests in one environment without interfering with your productive code. Enter the following code into the Anaconda Prompt, execute each line individually. These statements:

  • create a new Python 3.12 environment (which is currently the stable version of Python) named "sandbox"
  • activate the new "sandbox" environment, so that the following commands are executed for this environment
  • install the python package jupyterlab, which, you might have guessed, installs the Jupyter Notebook environment into the blank Python environment
  • add the "sandbox" envrionment to the Jupyter environment (you will see later, what this means)
  • and finally, open the Jupyter environment.

 

conda create --name sandbox python=3.12
conda activate sandbox
pip install jupyterlab
python -m ipykernel install --user --name=sandbox
jupyter lab

 

Executing these commands will open the Jupyter Lab environment, in which individual Jupyter Notebooks can be executed. Notice the "sandbox" icon in the Notebook section. This icon appears because of the earlier "python -m ipykernel install --user --name=sandbox" command. 

Click the "sandbox" icon in the Notebook section and a Jupyter Notebooks opens up, in which you can script your Python code. At the top right you can see that the Notebook is running in the "sandbox" environment. Eventually you might have a number of Python environments, hence it is good to know for sure, in which of these environments the code is executed.

In case you haven't worked with Jupyter Notebooks yet, this introduction to JupyterLab gets you started. Once you are familiar with the Jupyter interface, install the hana_ml package, that we will use to trigger the embedded Machine Learning. Enter this code into a cell in the Notebook and execute it, for example with the "Run" icon on top.

 

!pip install hana_ml

 

This downloads and install the hana_ml-package with its dependencies, which can take a few seconds. Once complete, run this code in a new cell, to see the version of the freshly installed package. 

 

import hana_ml
print(hana_ml.__version__)

 

After a successful installation you see a version such as "2.21.24071200".

Later on we will also need the matplotlib package when charting the numbers. Run this command in a new cell.

 

!pip install matplotlib

 

Time-series forecasting

We are ready for the fun part, everything should be ready to upload some data and create some time-series forecasts. Remember, that all code and data can be downloaded from this repository. 

Data upload

Begin by uploading the historic data, that we use to create a time-series forecast. Go into your space in SAP Datasphere. In the Data Builder use the "Import CSV File" option to upload the the file OVERNIGHTSTAYS.csv as local table. 

Keep the defaults when clicking through the steps to load the data. You will see a preview with its four columns.

  • The month
  • The Swiss region
  • The nationality of the guests
  • And the number of overnight stays 

 

Click "Deploy". Keep the default table name "OVERNIGHTSTAYS". Click "Deploy" again and the data is in a local table in SAP Datasphere.

So the data is in SAP Datasphere, but to make it visible and usable for our Database user, the data needs to be provided as a view, which is exposed for consumption.

  • Create a new "Graphical View"
  • Drag the table OVERNIGHTSTAYS onto the canvas
  • Set the Business Name of the view to: V_OVERNIGHTSTAYS
  • Activate the option "Expose for Consumption"

For many cases this would be sufficient to use the embedded Machine Learning on this data. In our case however the column OVERNIGHTSTAYS is of type Integer64, which becomes a BIGINT in the underlying SAP HANA Cloud. Since the Machine Learning algorithm that we want to use does not support BIGINT, we change the variable's type to Integer.

  • Add a "Calculated Columns" step to the Calculation View.
  • Go into the "Edit" mode of the OVERNIGHTSTAYS column
  • Change the expression to: TO_INTEGER(OVERNIGHTSTAYS)
  • Change the Data Type to: Integer

Now deploy the view and all is in place to use the Machine Learning.

 

Forecasting a time-series

You find this section's code in the Notebook 010 Time-series forecast.ipynb. 

Begin by logging on to SAP Datasphere's embedded SAP HANA Cloud, through the hana_ml package in a Jupyter Notebook. Use the credentials of the database user, the address is the database user's host name.

 

import hana_ml.dataframe as dataframe
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(address='YOURENDPOINT',
                                   port=443,
